one_line_takeaway: LR-DWM matches prior diffusion-LM watermarking methods (WM-DLM, DMARK) on detectability and text quality while avoiding their runtime or memory overhead, by biasing logits with independent left- and right-neighbor green-list hashes instead of inverting the hash function or caching a full vocabulary-squared lookup table.
key_finding: On LLaDA, at matched detection operating points (Table 1), LR-DWM achieves comparable or slightly better perplexity than the two baselines: at 90% detection, PPL is 2.80 +/- 0.46 for LR-DWM vs 2.82 +/- 0.51 for DMARK and 5.07 +/- 1.40 for WM-DLM; at 99% detection, 3.32 +/- 0.65 (LR-DWM) vs 3.28 +/- 0.61 (DMARK) vs 6.14 +/- 1.84 (WM-DLM); at 99.5% detection, 3.37 +/- 0.66 (LR-DWM) vs 3.34 +/- 0.63 (DMARK) vs 6.33 +/- 1.90 (WM-DLM). The claimed advantage is efficiency, not detectability/quality (those are described as merely 'competitive' with DMARK): per Figure 1, LR-DWM stays close to the unwatermarked deterministic baseline in both wall-clock time and peak GPU memory, while DMARK nearly doubles peak GPU memory due to caching its full |V|^2 hash table, and WM-DLM has higher runtime overhead from its expectation-based scoring under stochastic decoding. Robustness (Table 2, at delta=3.25, 1% FPR): detection stays high under light non-adaptive attacks -- 98.83% under 10% word deletion, 98.25% under 10% word substitution, 99.42% under 10% context-aware (BERT) substitution -- but degrades sharply under stronger perturbations, dropping to 59.06% at 50% word deletion, 51.46% at 50% word substitution, 55.56% under EN->ZH->EN back-translation, and 15.79% under LLM paraphrasing (Qwen-2B), the most damaging attack tested.
core_contribution: The paper introduces LR-DWM (Left-Right Diffusion Watermarking), a watermarking scheme for Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) that biases token logits using two independent hash-based 'green list' constraints derived from the left neighbor (key kL) and right neighbor (key kR) of a target position, whichever are already revealed at a given denoising step. The additive logit bias l'_v = l_v + delta*I(v in G_L) + delta*I(v in G_R) requires no inversion of the hashing function and no precomputed |V|^2 lookup table, unlike the two prior DLM watermarking methods it compares against. Detection uses a ternary per-token score s_i = m_L + m_R - 1 in {+1,0,-1}, summed and standardized (Z = sum(s_i)/(sigma*sqrt(T))) into a Z-score whose null variance is empirically calibrated on 10,000 human-written C4 texts (400 tokens each) to hit a target 1% FPR.
threat_model: Provenance/attribution setting, not an adversarial-attacker threat model in the classic sense: a content owner/model provider embeds a statistical signal at generation time (via secret hash keys kL, kR) so that a detector holding those keys can later determine whether a given text was produced by the watermarked DLM versus written by a human, at a calibrated false-positive rate. Robustness is evaluated only against standard, non-adaptive third-party text perturbations (an entity trying to strip or evade the watermark by editing the text) using the MARKLLM benchmark -- word deletion, random word substitution, context-aware (BERT) substitution, back-translation, and LLM paraphrasing -- not against an adaptive or watermark-aware adversary. The paper explicitly states this limitation itself.
limitations: Author-stated limitations: text-quality degradation, while small, can become non-negligible when very high detection rates are required; the scheme needs a minimal text length for the detection signal to be statistically significant; and robustness evaluation covers only standard non-adaptive text perturbations, not adversarial/watermark-aware attacks. The paper itself also notes paraphrasing sharply degrades detection because it disrupts the local bidirectional lexical context the method relies on, calling this 'a known limitation shared by watermarking methods based on local token correlations.' DREAM-7B results are reported as showing a larger LR-DWM vs DMARK gap than on LLaDA, which the authors attribute to decoding dynamics/filtering under low-temperature stochastic sampling rather than a change in watermark signal quality -- an explanation offered but not independently isolated experimentally. No comparison of detection-time computational cost (only generation-time cost) is reported, and no evaluation against adaptive attackers who know the watermarking scheme is described.

datasets_used: C4 corpus (10,000 human-written 400-token texts, used for null-hypothesis/FPR calibration)
quantitative_results: PPL at 90% detection (LLaDA): LR-DWM 2.80 +/- 0.46, DMARK 2.82 +/- 0.51, WM-DLM 5.07 +/- 1.40
PPL at 99% detection (LLaDA): LR-DWM 3.32 +/- 0.65, DMARK 3.28 +/- 0.61, WM-DLM 6.14 +/- 1.84
PPL at 99.5% detection (LLaDA): LR-DWM 3.37 +/- 0.66, DMARK 3.34 +/- 0.63, WM-DLM 6.33 +/- 1.90

Robustness at delta=3.25, 1% FPR (clean baseline: 100% detection, avg Z=6.653): 98.83% detection under 10% word deletion; 90.94% under 30% word deletion; 59.06% under 50% word deletion; 98.25% under 10% word substitution; 51.46% under 50% word substitution; 99.42% under 10% context-aware BERT substitution; 98.54% under 30% context-aware substitution; 55.56% under EN-ZH-EN back-translation; 15.79% under Qwen-2B paraphrasing
DMARK caches full |V|^2 lookup table causing near doubling of peak GPU memory vs LR-DWM/baseline
10,000 human-written C4 texts of 400 tokens used to calibrate 1% empirical FPR threshold
baselines_compared: WM-DLM (Gloaguen et al. 2025, stochastic expectation-based marginalization)
DMARK (Wu et al. 2025, order-agnostic inverse-hash kernel with |V|^2 cached lookup table)
